text: The patient is positioned supine with arms tucked, and the robotic right hemicolectomy begins with a standard port configuration. A gel point mini is placed in a 3 cm incision, along with one 12 mm port and two 8 mm ports. The small bowel is retracted proximally using an endoractor. Fenestrated bipolar forceps grasp the mesentery, while monopolar curved scissors dissect the peritoneum. The superior mesenteric vein (SMV) is exposed, and the iliocolic pedicle is dissected, with the iliocolic artery positioned posterior to the vein. The iliocolic vein and artery are divided together using mechanical fixation and an energy device. Three hemo-lock clips are applied, and the vessel sealer is used. The duodenum is dissected away and pushed posteriorly as the dissection continues along the SMV towards the pancreas.

The surgeon navigates the variations of Henle's trunk and its tributaries, focusing on the configuration where the superior right colic vein joins the right gastroepiploic vein and the anterior superior pancreatoduodenal vein. The dissection proceeds along the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), approaching the middle colic artery and its right branch. The duodenum and pancreas are visibly cleared of overlying lymphatic tissue, and the middle colic vein and the right branch of the middle colic artery are dissected.

The surgeon takes the right colic vein while performing a central mesocolic excision. The medial border of the excision is positioned over the superior mesenteric vein (SMV), and the superior border is defined by the junction of the gastroepiploic vein with the trunk of Henle. Surgery cell fibrilla is applied to control venous bleeding, avoiding energy devices. The dissection progresses medially to laterally, with the duodenum being carefully dissected.

The surgeon divides the lateral attachments of the ascending colon and uses a vessel sealer to divide the ileal mesentery. A SureForm 16mm blue load cartridge is employed to transect the ileum. The greater omentum is separated from the transverse colon, and the transverse colon mesentery is divided. The lesser sac is entered, and the lesser omentum is taken down. The dissection proceeds toward the hepatic flexure using a top-down approach. The colon is divided first to form the anastomosis, with a window created in the colonic mesentery using a Shaw Forms 60mm blue load cartridge.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the mesentery, placing the specimen above the liver for retrieval later. Firefly imaging post-ICG injection confirms the vascularity of the conduits. The bowel is positioned for an isoperistaltic side-to-side anastomosis, with the enterotomy on the small bowel made at least two centimeters from the staple line. The greater omentum is placed in the supracolic compartment. A colotomy is made in the tinea of the transverse colon, and a sixty millimeter blue load SureForm stapler is positioned with the small jaw in the mobile small bowel first, followed by the large jaw in the large bowel. Care is taken to avoid tearing the colon, aligning the enterotomy and colotomy evenly before firing the stapler. The common enterotomy is closed using a 3-0 V-Lock suture, with sutures pulled tight at the end of each throw and tied together in the middle. The anastomosis is covered with omentum.
